title: GUIDELINES PROCEDURES FOR THE VISUAL INSPECTION OF LOTS OF CANNED FOODS FOR UNACCEPTABLE 1  DEFECTS

sections.section: GUIDELINES PROCEDURES FOR THE VISUAL INSPECTION OF LOTS OF CANNED FOODS FOR UNACCEPTABLE 1  DEFECTS
sections.summary: Document title: Guidelines/procedures for the visual inspection of lots of canned foods for unacceptable defects.
Document code/reference: CAC/GL 17-1993.
Table of contents — lists the guide's sections (preface, introduction, objective, inspector, inspection, sampling, actions, appendix) and page references; non‑substantive as a content index.

Preface: states that canned‑food safety is primarily assured by GMPs and that when lot safety/acceptability is in question GMP adherence should be verified and, if unavailable, an expert should assess the lot using inspection and available documentation; it warns that visual inspection can detect some container defects and microbiological risk but is limited, should use statistically based sampling, and must not be the sole basis for judging fitness for consumption, with sorting and retention decisions made by experienced personnel.
Non‑substantive TOC/page entry referencing the Introduction section.

Introduction: characterizes the external container defects illustrated in the manual as visually obvious and rendering containers/contents unfit for sale, expects inspectors with minimal training to recognize and remove them, and emphasizes that sampling inspection gives limited assurance and must be considered alongside GMP verification, expert advice and regulatory requirements when determining actions.
Non‑substantive TOC/page entry referencing the Objective section.

Objective: directs this guide to those responsible for visually inspecting lots of canned foods for the unacceptable defects shown in the pictorial manual and Appendix 2, and states it is not intended to determine lot disposition.
Non‑substantive TOC/page entry referencing the Inspector section.
Defines 'inspector' broadly as any person charged with inspecting a lot of canned foods for container defects, not limited to regulatory agency personnel.
Training requirement: inspectors should be trained in the techniques for detecting and identifying the unacceptable container defects depicted in the manual and listed in Appendix 2.
Powers: inspectors should have authority to control a lot during inspection (including evaluating results) and to control disposal of defective cans or the entire lot if deemed unacceptable for sale.
Table of contents entry for Section 4 (Inspection); no substantive regulatory text in this item.

Describes inspection of canned-food lots, covering preparation (information to provide to the inspector, accessibility, lighting and magnification, reference defect manual and checklist) and an overview inspection procedure to detect, segregate, record and manage damaged, wetted or stained cartons including when 100% inspection or removal is appropriate.

Specifies preparation for inspection: supply the inspector with detailed lot information (location, lot size, food and can type, code lots, processor/origin, sampling plan), ensure full access, adequate space/illumination and tools (3x–5x magnifier, high‑intensity light), have the reference defect manual available, provide assistance for access, and use a checklist/form to record required observations and sampling details.

Requires a visual overview of the lot to identify damaged, wetted or stained cartons, to expose as many cartons as possible, to separate and record such cartons and their locations, to prevent their removal until inspected, to allow removal for localized transport damage if not prevailing, to refer non‑handling damage to Section 6, and to subject separated cartons to 100% inspection if appropriate.
Table of contents entry for Section 5 (Sampling Inspection); no substantive regulatory text in this item.

Requires sampling of lots according to a designated sampling plan (to be recorded), use of random/statistical selection where appropriate, ensuring samples are representative and accessible, separately handling multiple code lots as decided beforehand, and identifying and recording each sample unit and the number taken.

Directs careful examination of selected cans for external defects (swelling, leakage, product evidence, label staining), marking and removing labels from suspect cans for closer inspection (focusing on seams, embossing and tear strips), recording all observed defects including unacceptable ones, and seeking expert opinion when uncertain.
Table of contents entry for Section 6 (Action When Defects Are Found); no substantive regulatory text in this item.

When an inspector finds any defect listed in the manual they must notify a superior or follow established procedures, may retain the lot and send defective cans for laboratory investigation, and must exercise care in handling, shipping or disposing of defective cans which should remain under control until destroyed.

Provides an Appendix I lot inspection record template listing required lot information (owner/consignee, location, manufacturer, transport, arrival date, carton/container counts, product and packaging, container type/size, code lots and documentation), inspection details (date, inspector, sampling plan/method, sample ID, defects, lab results, observations) and disposition items (accept/detain, disposal of unacceptable containers, recommended or taken actions).

sections.section: 1 Unacceptable defects are those which show visual evidence that a metal container is without hermetic seal or that microbial growth has occurred in the container's contents (see Appendix 2).
sections.summary: Defines 'unacceptable defects' as visible evidence that a metal container lacks a hermetic seal or that microbial growth has occurred in its contents.

Explains that the pictorial manual and Appendix 2 list obvious external container defects that render canned foods unsuitable for sale, that minimally trained inspectors should remove such containers and seek expert advice for the remainder, and that inspection of samples provides limited assurance compared with adherence to Good Manufacturing Practices, with disposition depending on defect quantity/type and regulatory requirements.

States the guide's objective: to assist those responsible for visual inspection of canned-food lots for the unacceptable defects depicted in the pictorial manual, and clarifies it is not intended to determine lot disposition.
Defines 'inspector' as anyone charged with inspecting lots of canned foods for container defects and not limited to regulatory agency personnel.

Requires inspectors to be trained in inspection techniques for canned-food container defects, with emphasis on detecting and identifying the unacceptable defects depicted in the manual and listed in Appendix 2.

States that inspectors should have authority to control a lot until inspection and evaluation are complete, including authority over disposal of defective cans and the lot if deemed unacceptable for sale.

Describes inspection procedures for canned-food lots, including preparatory information to provide the inspector, requirements for accessibility and inspection tools, use of a checklist, and an overview inspection to detect, segregate, record and manage damaged, wetted or stained cartons with possible 100% inspection or referral to Section 6 for non‑handling damage.

Specifies preparatory requirements for inspection: give the inspector detailed lot information (location, lot size, food and can type, list of codes, processor/country/legal agent, sampling plan), ensure access, space and lighting, use magnification and reference manuals as needed, provide assistance for sampling, and use a checklist to record observations and sampling details.

Requires an overview visual inspection for damaged, wetted or stained cartons, exposure of as many cartons as possible, separation and recording of suspect cartons and their locations, precautions to prevent removal until inspected, allowance for removal when damage is clearly from handling and not pervasive, referral to Section 6 for non‑handling damage, and potential 100% inspection of separated cartons.

Mandates sampling lots according to a recorded sampling plan, using random/statistical selection when appropriate, ensuring representativeness and access, deciding whether to sample multiple code lots separately, and identifying and recording each sample unit and the sample size.

Procedure for examining selected cans: carefully observe external appearance for swelling or leakage (including product/stain evidence), mark and remove labels from suspect cans to locate defects, inspect all parts (seams, embossing, tear strips), record every defect for each sample unit and note unacceptable defects per the manual, and seek an expert second opinion if uncertain.

When an inspector finds defects listed in the manual he must notify his superior or follow established procedures which may include retaining the lot and sending defective cans to a laboratory, handling/shipping/disposing of potentially hazardous defective cans with care, and keeping all defective cans under control until destroyed.

Required lot information to record includes owner/consignee and location, manufacturer details, transport and arrival date, carton and container counts, product identification and secondary packaging, container type/size, code lots and interpretation, accompanying documents, salvage status, whether the lot is part of a larger consignment, and location of any remainder.

Required inspection records include date and inspector identity/affiliation, sampling plan and method, whether sampling access was free, number and identification of sample units, detailed defects per container with unacceptable defects noted, containers sent to laboratory and laboratory results, plus other comments or observations.

Disposition records must state whether the lot was accepted or detained, describe how defective/unacceptable containers were disposed of, and indicate recommended or taken further actions if the lot was retained.
This entry is only the heading 'LOT INSPECTION RECORD' and contains no substantive content.

Lists unacceptable can defects that define rejection criteria, including perforated external corrosion, severe body or double-seam denting/fracture with leakage, defective or incomplete side seam welds (including mislocks and leaks), body punctures/perforations and hard/buckle/blown swells.

Appendix listing additional unacceptable can defects (items 12–20) such as cable-cut endplate fractures with leakage, embossed-code fractures, deadhead/skidder, incomplete double seam, cut-through fractures, torn flanges/curls and related end/curl damage.
